content.text: Structured ideation workshops are the primary mechanism enterprises use to surface high-value agent and GenAI use cases from domain experts who would not otherwise articulate them through a requirements document. Unlike generic design thinking workshops, GenAI ideation sessions must account for capability boundaries unique to language models — hallucination risk, data sensitivity, latency constraints, and the agentic vs. assistive distinction — so participants understand what they are designing for before ideas are captured.

Without a facilitated discovery process, GenAI programs default to two failure modes. The first is bottom-up enthusiasm: individual developers prototype the ideas they personally find interesting, producing a portfolio of disconnected demos that never reach production. The second is top-down mandate: leadership instructs teams to apply GenAI to a predefined list of processes, skipping the discovery phase that would reveal whether those processes are actually amenable to automation.

A well-run ideation workshop corrects both failure modes. It surfaces use cases from the people closest to the work — process owners, analysts, customer service teams — and subjects each idea to rapid cross-functional scrutiny before any code is written. The output is not a wishlist; it is a structured backlog of candidate use cases with preliminary value hypotheses, data dependencies, and risk flags, ready for the scoring and triage phases that follow.
Frontline staff know where the friction is. Structured facilitation translates tacit process knowledge into actionable use-case descriptions that engineering teams can evaluate.
Participants who helped generate the backlog are more likely to sponsor and champion PoC work than those who receive a list handed down from above.
Business and technology stakeholders align on what "agentic" means and where the model's limitations apply, reducing misaligned expectations during build.
With good output templates, the raw backlog is usable the same day — no post-processing lag waiting for a consultant to write it up.
Data privacy, regulatory constraints, and hallucination exposure are easier to identify when domain experts and legal or compliance representatives are in the room together.
A half-day workshop with 12 participants generates more candidate use cases than three weeks of individual interviews and documentation review.

Workshops are expensive in terms of senior time. A four-hour session with 12 participants represents 48 person-hours of organizational cost. If the backlog produced is not acted upon — no scoring, no PoC sponsorship, no roadmap entry — participants feel the exercise was performative, and future workshops become hard to staff.
Early vocal participants can anchor the group on a narrow set of ideas. Silent brainstorming before group discussion is the mitigation.

A polished GenAI demo at the start of the workshop sets an implicit quality bar that inflates participants' confidence in what is buildable. Calibrate the demo to show realistic limitations alongside capabilities.
Workshops staffed only with business stakeholders produce ideas that are technically infeasible or ignore integration complexity. At least one ML engineer and one data engineer should attend.
Ideas that emerge without a named champion and a sponsoring team rarely survive the transition from backlog to PoC. Assign ownership during the workshop, not as a post-processing step.

An effective GenAI ideation workshop has three phases: pre-workshop preparation, the facilitated workshop session, and post-workshop output capture. Each phase has distinct deliverables and failure points.

Send a pain-point survey one week before the session, asking participants to identify three to five repetitive, time-consuming tasks that require judgment or synthesis. This primes participants and prevents the first 30 minutes of the workshop from being spent excavating process knowledge under time pressure.

Distribute a one-page briefing sheet on GenAI capabilities and known limitations relevant to your enterprise context: what the model can and cannot reliably do, what data it will and will not be allowed to see, and what "agentic" means in practice versus a simpler copilot pattern. Without this calibration, participants generate ideas appropriate for a magic system, not a real one.

Ensure participant diversity: at least one business process owner, one domain expert, one data or analytics professional, one technology architect or ML engineer, and one representative from legal or compliance for high-risk domains. Cross-functional tension in ideation is a feature — it surfaces feasibility and risk concerns that would otherwise emerge weeks later.

Open with an icebreaker question: "Name one thing AI already does for you or your team today, however small." This normalizes GenAI as practical rather than futuristic and reveals the group's baseline familiarity.

Run a 10-minute silent brainstorm before any group discussion. Each participant writes one idea per sticky note (physical or digital). Silence prevents anchoring and ensures the group hears from introverts. The facilitator then reads all notes aloud before any discussion begins.

Cluster notes into affinity groups by process domain (e.g., customer support, finance, supply chain). Then apply "How Might We" reframes to sharpen vague ideas into solvable problem statements: "How might we reduce the time analysts spend synthesizing weekly reports?" rather than "AI for reports."

For each top cluster, run a rapid straw-man scenario: describe what the system would actually do step by step. This forces the group to distinguish between an agentic pattern (autonomous, multi-step, tool-using) and an assistive pattern (draft-then-review, copilot). Early misclassification inflates scope and sets unrealistic timelines.
Close with dot-voting: each participant gets five votes to allocate across ideas. Votes reveal preference but do not determine the final shortlist — that requires formal scoring in the next phase.

Two techniques are specific to GenAI discovery and are not present in standard design thinking workshops. First, a "hallucination exposure" exercise: for each idea, ask the group what happens when the model confidently produces a wrong answer. Does a human review the output before it is acted on? What is the worst-case downstream effect? Ideas where wrong answers would cause significant harm without detection need human-in-the-loop safeguards designed upfront.

Second, a "data sensitivity triage" step: for each idea, identify what data the agent would need to read, and what data it might write or expose in its responses. This is not a full data governance review — it is a rapid flag-raising exercise to mark ideas that require legal or compliance sign-off before a PoC can proceed.

attrs.alt: GenAI Agent Ideation Workshop flow: four phases — Pre-Workshop preparation, Workshop Day facilitation, Output Capture, and Post-Workshop actions — each with their key activities listed.

content.text: The four phases map onto calendar time: pre-workshop preparation takes one to two weeks, the workshop itself is a half-day to full day, output capture happens in parallel during the session, and post-workshop actions take approximately one week before the backlog is in a state suitable for formal scoring.
starting a new GenAI program and the organization does not yet have a candidate use-case backlog.
entering a new business domain (e.g., moving from customer service to finance automation) where process knowledge is held by a different team.
the existing backlog has stalled and no new ideas are being generated — a workshop reinjects domain expertise and stakeholder energy.
the team already has a rich, scored backlog and the constraint is delivery capacity rather than idea generation.
the problem is already well-defined and bounded. A two-hour requirements session with the process owner is more efficient than a full ideation workshop for narrow, pre-scoped problems.
Identify a target business domain and secure sponsor commitment to act on workshop outputs.
Draft the participant list: aim for 8–14 people with the cross-functional mix described above. More than 14 requires multiple breakout groups and a skilled facilitation team.
Send the pain-point survey 7–10 days in advance. Analyze responses to identify recurring themes and prepare clusters as a facilitation aid.
Prepare the capability briefing sheet and a 10-minute live demo (or recorded demo) calibrated to realistic enterprise use cases in the domain.
Prepare the output template: one canvas per use case capturing problem statement, value hypothesis, agentic/assistive classification, data dependencies, risk flags, and proposed owner.
Run the workshop: icebreaker → silent brainstorm → cluster → HMW reframe → straw-man scenarios → hallucination triage → data sensitivity triage → dot-vote.
Complete canvases for the top 10 ideas during the workshop, using a scribe or the participants themselves.
Within two days, distribute the raw backlog to all participants for additions and corrections. Assign owners during this review.
Move the finalized backlog into the scoring phase (weighted scoring model) within one week.

# use_case_canvas.yaml — one file per idea captured during workshop
use_case_id: UC-2024-031
title: "Automated RFP response drafting"
problem_statement: >
  Sales teams spend 6–12 hours per RFP gathering input from product, legal,
  and pricing, then assembling a compliant response document. Process is
  repetitive and error-prone when policies change.
value_hypothesis:
  time_saving_hours_per_rfp: 5
  rfp_volume_per_year: 200
  annual_hours_saved: 1000
  additional_value: "Faster response → higher win rate (estimated +5%)"
pattern: agentic          # agentic | assistive | undecided
agent_steps:
  - "Retrieve relevant policy and product docs from knowledge base"
  - "Draft section responses using retrieved context"
  - "Flag missing information requiring human input"
  - "Assemble structured output in RFP template format"
human_review_required: true
review_stage: "Before submission — human approves final draft"
data_dependencies:
  - source: "SharePoint RFP library"
    sensitivity: internal
  - source: "Product specs database"
    sensitivity: internal
  - source: "Customer details"
    sensitivity: pii-low
risk_flags:
  - "Hallucinated policy details could cause compliance issues"
  - "Customer name exposure in multi-tenant deployment"
owner: "Jane Smith, Sales Operations"
sponsor: "VP Sales"
workshop_votes: 8
parking_lot: false

A global insurance carrier ran a two-day GenAI ideation program across its claims, underwriting, and customer service divisions. Each division received a half-day workshop with a cross-functional group of 12, facilitated by an internal AI team with an external delivery partner.

The pre-workshop pain-point survey surfaced 47 distinct friction points across the three divisions. The workshops produced 38 candidate use cases over two days. The hallucination triage exercise eliminated 6 ideas outright (high-stakes decisions where automated error was deemed unacceptable without existing human oversight controls). The data sensitivity triage flagged 9 ideas requiring legal review before PoC. The remaining 23 entered the scoring phase.

Of those 23, 5 reached PoC stage within the following quarter. Two of those 5 reached production within 12 months. The workshop outputs were traced directly to production value because the canvas format captured value hypotheses that could be validated against PoC results and then used to build the full business case.
Starting with the Technology, Not the Problem

Workshops framed as "what can we do with GenAI?" produce answers that start from the technology and work backward to a problem. The workshop should begin with process pain points and work forward to the capability. "We waste four hours per analyst per week reconciling these two data sources" is a better starting point than "we should use RAG."
Skipping the Hallucination and Data Triage Steps

These steps feel like they slow the workshop down. They are non-negotiable for enterprise GenAI discovery. Ideas that skip risk triage in the workshop re-encounter the same risks weeks later in legal review, compliance sign-off, or security assessment — at much higher cost and later in the PoC lifecycle.

A workshop that ends with a backlog but no agreed next step has a high probability of the backlog sitting untouched for months. The last 15 minutes of every workshop must be used to assign owners, name a scoring lead, and set a date for the scoring session. Without this, the output has no pull.
Always run individual silent ideation before group discussion. Ten minutes of independent writing generates more diverse ideas than open brainstorming and avoids groupthink.
Show a realistic enterprise use case in the target domain, and deliberately include at least one example of model failure or limitation. Over-promising in the opening demo leads to scope inflation.
Coming in with seed clusters from the survey analysis accelerates the affinity clustering step and validates survey findings with the live group.
For every top-voted idea, the group must articulate what the system does step by step. This converts abstract ideas into concrete system behaviors and reveals infeasibility early.
Every use case that exits the workshop must have a named owner and a named sponsor. Anonymous ideas do not become PoCs.

Ideas that cannot be evaluated due to missing information should go to a visible parking lot during the session, not be discarded. Review the parking lot in the post-workshop session — some ideas become viable once a missing constraint is resolved.
Distribute the raw backlog to all participants within two business days. Momentum dissipates quickly; a two-week turnaround on notes signals to participants that the workshop was not taken seriously.

For large enterprises spanning multiple divisions, a single workshop does not provide adequate coverage. Run domain-specific workshops per business unit and then hold a cross-division synthesis session to identify shared infrastructure, common data sources, and overlapping use cases that can be built once and reused.

Reuse the pre-workshop survey infrastructure across workshops. After the first workshop, the survey becomes richer — add questions informed by emerging themes from earlier workshops. Over time, the survey data produces longitudinal insight into which process domains generate the most ideas and which have the highest PoC conversion rate.
(MIRO, Mural, FigJam) scale better than physical sticky notes for distributed teams and produce structured digital artifacts that feed directly into backlog tooling.
Export canvas data to JSON and ingest directly into a scoring script or a project management tool (Jira, Azure DevOps) to eliminate manual transfer and data loss.

Train internal facilitators after the first workshop run by an external partner. Internalized facilitation is more sustainable and less expensive than relying on consultants for every subsequent workshop.
— digital whiteboard tools for remote or hybrid workshops. Support sticky notes, voting, and affinity mapping. Export to structured formats.
— pre-workshop survey distribution. Typeform's conditional logic allows pain-point probing based on role.
— canvas template hosting and post-workshop backlog storage. Version-controlled and linkable to PoC documentation.
— downstream backlog management. Import workshop outputs as epics or tickets with custom fields matching the canvas schema.
— survey response analysis, theme clustering, and backlog scoring scripts. Lightweight enough to run without data infrastructure.

Canvas outputs contain business-sensitive information: process descriptions, data source names, value hypotheses, and sometimes preliminary estimates of cost or revenue impact. Store canvas artifacts in access-controlled repositories, not open shared drives. Limit distribution of the raw backlog to named participants and sponsors until the scoring phase.
Do not use GenAI tools to transcribe or analyze workshop notes without first confirming that the tool's data handling meets enterprise data classification requirements.

If digital whiteboard tools are used, ensure they are approved for the data sensitivity level of information being discussed. Some enterprise domains (healthcare, defense) may require on-premises tools.
Version-control the canvas templates so that changes to the schema are tracked and backward-compatible with earlier workshop outputs.
Think of the ideation workshop as a

. The organization's process knowledge is a rock face containing veins of genuine value, but the veins are not visible from the surface and are distributed unevenly across domains and teams. The workshop is the systematic excavation: pre-survey identifies which areas of the rock face are most promising, silent brainstorming extracts raw material from multiple locations simultaneously, affinity clustering separates ore from rock, and the straw-man scenario assessment assays each sample for real value. The parking lot preserves material whose value cannot yet be assessed. What you carry out of the dig is not finished product — it is feedstock for the scoring and triage processes that convert raw ideas into a prioritized delivery roadmap.
Pre-workshop preparation — pain-point survey, capability briefing, and participant diversity — determines output quality more than workshop facilitation technique.
Silent brainstorming before group discussion prevents anchoring and produces more diverse ideas from more participants.
Straw-man scenarios during the workshop convert abstract ideas into concrete step-by-step system behaviors, revealing infeasibility and agentic vs. assistive classification simultaneously.
Hallucination exposure and data sensitivity triage are GenAI-specific steps that must be embedded in the workshop, not deferred to later discovery phases.
Every idea that exits the workshop must have a named owner and sponsor; anonymous ideas do not survive the transition from backlog to PoC.
The workshop output is a structured use-case canvas backlog, not a slideshow of themes — canvases feed directly into the scoring phase without additional processing.
Follow-up within 48 hours: distribute the raw backlog and confirm next steps before participant momentum dissipates.
